title: The black budget - What I think

transcript.text: Hello, this video is what I think about the black budget of America.
I did a video yesterday, last night, of what I think about the new world order,
And then I realized that, really, I think, for me,
The meat of the whole conversation about that has to do with the black budget of America.
That's what you want to do.
If you're going to be like, no to the new world order, you should be like, no to the black budget.
It has kind of like a couple of different facets to it.
One of it is, well, I mean, it's transparency.
We need transparency.
If you want to fight the new world order, you need freedom to buy drugs,
The drugs you need without a doctor's prescription or the approval of the FDA,
Because that's just mafia right there.
That's just another way to only let certain people make money off stuff.
And so, you know, you want to start holographically.
You want to start with yourself by just the little things, just like saying,
Just tattooing marijuana plants on your arm, that kind of shit.
Smoking weed on YouTube.
Giving the guy at the pharmacy a hard time for not selling you an inhaler without a prescription, you know.
Call him another minion for the Nazis or something, you know.
It's like, give me some fucking freedom here.
Sell me a goddamn inhaler.
I don't need a doctor to tell me that I have asthma.
I know I have asthma.
That kind of shit.
But there's like transparency, though, also.
So, like, where does the money go?
There's a woman who was interviewed on Coast to Coast that I heard a couple years ago.
Linda something or other, who is the director of housing under, I think, the Clinton administration.
And she said that there was $17,000 a year of American citizens' money goes disappearing every single year.
And the books, you know, don't add up with the United States.
And I think everybody would be able to tell you that.
But, and there's also, I'd like to read the book, but there's a book, I think, I can't remember,
But the guy who started LEAP Law Enforcing Against Prohibition.
I could say Jack Kemp said he read a book that, like, the Vietnam War was all about selling, making money on heroin.
And that, like, George Bush, you know, imported heroin.
Or maybe somebody else said that, made money importing drugs to America.
Okay. But, and that's another reason why weed is illegal and drugs are illegal in America,
Is so the government can make money on selling them.
But one thing that's pretty clear to me that's going on is there are, there's, we're building tunnels underneath the ground.
And I suppose that we have tunnels that are crisscrossing and connecting the entire United States.
And, you know, we're building flying saucers and stuff, back engineering flying saucers.
And it's just trillions and trillions of dollars of our money that we're paying for that.
But what does that mean to the average person?
And, well, the reason I know that is from all the people talking about it on the Disclosure Project.
And I saw some other interviews about people who have been down there.
One guy got in a laser battle with a bunch of aliens in the cavern they went into.
And there isn't actually even a couple of interviews I've seen with, like, three people who were, like, government assassins.
And they were talking about the kind of stuff they did.
And, um, but, I mean, like, how could you make a government assassin?
That's another thing.
Change the education, but, um, but, uh, anyways, how, what does that mean to the average normal human being?
Like, how do you change that?
Well, you need to make the, you need to, you need to make the way we spend our money transparent.

I went over to the United States Department of Agriculture website, and I tried to, and I snooped around, and I tried to find out, you know, how much money we spend on the United States Department of Agriculture.
Well, that's not on there.
I wanted to know how much money, uh, we subsidize the cattle industry.
Uh, because I heard we subsidize the cattle industry.
Somebody mentioned that.
Um, you know, there's this whole subsidies program.
Well, there's nothing about that, so I actually write them.
And I get it, I get a letter back saying, oh, thanks for writing us, you know, how nice you write us.
And I don't remember anything else, but they didn't answer my goddamn question.
So, basically, what we need to do as citizens, and what I'm going to do, that's going to be my next project.
I'm going to have to psych myself up for it, though, because it's going to be a huge, huge, huge project.
Um, and, and, and, and I'm going to probably end up just banging my head against the wall.
Um, and the best thing to do would probably be to, like, get a law degree, or, like, run for office, and where you can actually do something.
But I want to find out how fucking much money we spend on shit.
I mean, don't you think, if you go to a website, like, if I was a, if I was a guy, if I was, like, the politician, or the head of one of these U.S. departments,
I'd put the, I'd put how much money we spend on shit on the website.
It's like, our budget is this much.
And if you know of a website or somewhere where it says that, then tell me, because I didn't see it anywhere.
And I think maybe you can find out what the budget of the actual department is.
But, but what about the departments within the department?
How about, you know, how much money we give to the cattle industry?
Where's that?
I looked everywhere.
I didn't freaking find it.
So, if you know where it is, please tell me.
But, um, that stuff should be right on the front page of the website.
It should be easy to find.
It shouldn't have, you shouldn't have to be Sherlock Holmes to find out how much money we subsidize the cattle,
How much money we subsidize this, how much money this program gets, how much money that program gets.
And you know what?
They should, with our government, we should be able to have how much money we tax on this.
It should be simple arithmetic.
How much money we, government spends on this and taxes you on this.
And, you know, any kid should be able to whip out a calculator and tally it up.
And the number should be exact at the end of the day.
Just like at the end of the day, the cash register person tallies up how much money they went through in and out of their cash register.
And, you know, and it should be within the same to a matter of sense.
Um, it's just ridiculous that, that you can't, you don't know how much money.
It's like, it's like, yeah, great website.
You figured out how to put a logo on the website, you know.
And you can make the links work.
You know, look, a photo of the director.
Well, what about how much money that's being spent on?
You know, I mean, I mean, and that's, that's just the beginning, you know.
Because, because you need, it's called intelligence.
You need to make an informative decision.
An informed decision about shit.
Okay.
And how can you do that if you don't know how much money we're spending on stuff, you know.
Um, if we all knew how much money we were spending on stuff, then we could talk about how much money we should be spending on stuff.
Because we know how much money we already are spending on stuff.
It's like, duh.
Um, um, I mean, I'd like to say that we're spending too much money on the cattle industry.
And that we should put more money into organic farms and, and, um, and, uh, you know, fruit and vegetable farms.
And even maybe, uh, hydroponic, uh, building, farming.
But I can't say shit because I don't know how much money we're spending on all that stuff because it's not up on the website.
And that's just a travesty.
Now, that's what I mean about the black budget and any kind of new world order, whatever.
Just, just start by making shit transparent.
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to put up on my website how much money we spend on stuff.
And I'm just going to fight, put as much up.
I'm going to have to do piecemeal, I guess.
Go to this website, find out how much they spent on that in that year.
Go to that website, find out how much and just piece it all together.
Just how I made my, uh, my timeline.
Like, by, by choosing all the, all these different timelines from here, there, next place and just piecing it all together.
Um, because the fuckers and, uh, government, I guess.
You know, can't see past their nose, you know.
Well, why would you put how much money?
Because if you put how much money, then the people can, like I said, you could talk about it.
Um, and then, you know, you would integrate that into the educational system.
Um, you'd have kids.
You'd raise kids in school to go, oh, yeah.
We spend this much money on that.
Well, I don't think we should spend that much money on that.
And so then you'd have, like, approval ratings, you know, on everything.
Like, right now we have approval ratings on the president.
Well, we should have approval ratings on everything.
We should have approval ratings on how much money we spend on each and every department.
Uh, approval ratings on each and every regulation.
And another thing, bills.
If you want to find out what bills are going through, that's like trying to read hieroglyphics.
If you go into the, uh, the, uh, the, uh, the government website, it's like, it's like each bill, it's got, like, these weird numbers in there.
And then, and then I heard this new resolution, um, to make the EU or whatever, 70,000 pages long.
I mean, if some, if you're going to make something that long, you're going to be able to sneak whatever the fuck you want in there and make it your own law.
Because nobody's going to read that much.
It doesn't have to be that much.
So it has to be, like, really, you know, concise and succinct and easy to, um, easy to read.
And it's just totally fucked up.
I mean, that's the kind of shit that makes me think that people were politicians were a bunch of retards.
Um, so it's just all about transparency and that's my next.
That's, that's, that's, that's, that's, that's the nuts and nuts and bolts of it.
But, here's my other time.
